style: Dry red, medium/full body, red/black fruit, oak-influenced, vanilla, spice, tertiary development.

conclusions: Ready to drink; can age further 5-10 years

common_student_errors: Underestimating the complexity from controlled oak ageing
Not recognizing American vs French oak differences

misconceptions_addressed.misconception: Oak spice and vanilla mean sweetness
misconceptions_addressed.correction: Oak-derived aromas are winemaking effects, not residual sugar; wine remains dry
Oak is positive when integrated and balanced; dominant oak indicates poor integration
Tertiary aromas (leather, tobacco) are positive indicators of complexity and bottle age

coaching_hints: Separate primary, secondary, and tertiary descriptors.
Is oak integrated or does it dominate?
What indicates quality in an oaked red?
How does oak ageing support structure rather than mask it?

causal_reasoning_paths.effect: Vanilla, spice, and oak-influenced aromas; increased complexity
causal_reasoning_paths.example_in_wine: American oak gives coconut; French oak gives subtler spice

causal_reasoning_paths.effect: Oak spice softens; tertiary aromas develop; integration improves
causal_reasoning_paths.example_in_wine: Wine released after oak is already integrated

causal_reasoning_paths.effect: Longer oak ageing than modern international style
causal_reasoning_paths.example_in_wine: Reserva shows pronounced oak and development
